notes.text: The Corleone family leads a powerful crime syndicate in 1940s New York. Michael Corleone, the youngest son, is a decorated war hero who wants nothing to do with the family business.
Michael Corleone, the reluctant heir, is intelligent, reserved, and moralistic. He sees himself outside the criminal world.
Theme of family vs. criminal enterprise: the Corleone family is bound by blood and violence.
Virgil 'The Turk' Sollozzo asks Don Vito Corleone to enter the narcotics trade. Don Corleone refuses, leading to an assassination attempt on his life.
The family needs Michael's help. He is called back from his 'ordinary world' of civilian life.
Michael initially resists involvement. He tells Kay, 'That's my family, Kay, not me.' He insists he is different.
Michael's internal conflict: loyalty to family vs. personal morality. The refusal highlights his tragic innocence.
Don Vito Corleone, recovering from the assassination attempt, becomes Michael's mentor in power and pragmatism. He teaches Michael how 'the man behind the table' operates.
Tom Hagen and Sonny also act as mentors in the family's violent code. Michael learns the rules of the criminal world.
Michael volunteers to kill Sollozzo and corrupt police captain McCluskey at a restaurant meeting. He crosses from civilian into murderer.
Michael's transformation begins: he retrieves a hidden gun, and his face becomes cold and determined.
Iconic line: 'It's not personal, Sonny. It's strictly business.'
Michael hides in Sicily. He meets and marries Apollonia. Meanwhile, Sonny is murdered at a causeway tollbooth.
Tom Hagen, loyal consigliere, remains an ally. The five families, especially Barzini and Tattaglia, are enemies.
Virgil Sollozzo and McCluskey are early enemies (shadows). Barzini is the ultimate puppet-master shadow.
Theme of loyalty and betrayal: the family is torn apart by internal and external enemies.
Michael returns to New York after Apollonia is killed by a car bomb meant for him. He prepares to take over as Don and plans a series of revenge assassinations.
Michael hardens completely. He lies to Kay about becoming 'legitimate' while secretly planning the purge.
During his nephew's baptism, Michael renounces Satan while his men murder the five family heads, Moe Greene, and others. This is his moral death.
The baptism scene cross-cut with murders: Michael becomes the new Godfather, losing his soul.
Theme of corruption: religious sanctity juxtaposed with brutal murder. Michael's 'ordeal' is spiritual damnation.
Michael eliminates all enemies. He becomes the undisputed Don of the most powerful crime family in New York. The 'reward' is power, but at the cost of his humanity.
Michael's reward: control. But he loses his innocence, his wife Kay, and his own conscience.
Kay confronts Michael about the murders. He denies ordering the killing of Carlo, who betrayed Sonny. But we see Michael's men close the door on Kay, excluding her.
Michael lies to Kay's face, then accepts the title of Don from his men. The road back is a lie.
Michael orders Carlo's murder. He has become what he once despised. The final resurrection is not of Michael the man, but of the new Godfather.
The old Michael is dead. The resurrected Michael is a cold, ruthless mafia boss.
Final shot: a door closes on Kay, symbolizing Michael's total isolation.
Michael returns with the 'elixir' of absolute power. He is now the new Don, having secured the family's future through violence and betrayal.
The tragic elixir: power corrupts absolutely. Michael has everything and nothing.
